html:
X

The Cloakroom Ticket

The sun woke Bernard. He rose from his bench with a violent headache. His gallant courage of the morning had left him. He felt abominably lonely and his heart was swelling with something brackish and bitter which he would not call unhappiness, but which brought the tears to his eyes. What should he do? Where should he go?тБатАКтБатАж If his steps turned towards St.┬аLazare Station at the time that he knew Olivier was due there, it was without any definite purpose and merely with the wish to see his friend again. He reproached himself for having left so abruptly that morning; perhaps Olivier had been hurt?тБатАКтБатАж Was he not the creature in the world he liked best?тБатАКтБатАж When he saw him arm in arm with Edouard a peculiar feeling made him follow the pair and at the same time not show himself; painfully conscious of being de trop, he would yet have liked to slip in between them. He thought Edouard looked charming; only a little taller than Olivier and with a scarcely less youthful figure. It was he whom he made up his mind to address; he would wait until Olivier left him. But address him? Upon what pretext?

It was at this moment that he caught sight of the little bit of crumpled paper as it escaped from EdouardтАЩs hand. He picked it up, saw that it was a cloakroom ticketтБатАКтБатАж and, by Jove, here was the wished-for pretext!

He saw the two friends go into a caf├й, hesitated a moment in perplexity, and then continued his monologue:

тАЬNow a normal fathead would have nothing better to do than to return this paper at once,тАЭ he said to himself.

тАЬтАКтАШHow weary, stale, flat and unprofitable
Seem to me all the uses of this world!тАЩ

as I have heard Hamlet remark. Bernard, Bernard, what thought is this that is tickling you? It was only yesterday that you were rifling a drawer. On what path are you entering? Consider, my boy, consider.тБатАКтБатАж Consider that the cloakroom attendant who took EdouardтАЩs luggage will be gone to his lunch at twelve oтАЩclock, and that there will be another one on duty. And didnтАЩt you promise your friend to stick at nothing?тАЭ

He reflected, however, that too much haste might spoil everything. The attendant might be surprised into thinking this haste suspicious; he might consult the entry book and think it unnatural that a piece of luggage deposited in the cloakroom a few minutes before twelve, should be taken out immediately after. And besides, suppose some passerby, some busybody, had seen him pick up the bit of paper.тБатАКтБатАж Bernard forced himself to walk to the Place de la Concorde without hurryingтБатАФin the time it would have taken another person to lunch. It is quite usual, isnтАЩt it, to put oneтАЩs luggage in the cloakroom whilst one is lunching and to take it out immediately after.тБатАКтБатАж His headache had gone. As he was passing by a restaurant terrace, he boldly took a toothpick from one of the little bundles that were set out on the tables, and stood nibbling it at the cloakroom counter, in order to give himself the air of having lunched. He was lucky to have in his favour his good looks, his well-cut clothes, his distinction, the frankness of his eyes and smile, and that indefinable something in the whole appearance which denotes those who have been brought up in comfort and want for nothing. (But all this gets rather draggled by sleeping on benches.)тБатАКтБатАж

He had a horrible turn when the attendant told him there were ten centimes to pay. He had not a single sou left. What should he do? The suitcase was there, on the counter. The slightest sign of hesitation would give the alarmтБатАФso would his want of money. But the demon is watching over him; he slips between BernardтАЩs anxious fingers, as they go searching from pocket to pocket with a pretence of feigned despair, a fifty-centime bit, which had lain forgotten since goodness knows when in his waistcoat pocket. Bernard hands it to the attendant. He has not shown a sign of his agitation. He takes up the suitcase, and in the simplest, honestest fashion pockets the forty centimes change. Heavens! How hot he is! Where shall he go now? His legs are beginning to fail him and the suitcase feels heavy. What shall he do with it?тБатАКтБатАж He suddenly remembers that he has no key. No! No! Certainly not! He will not break open the lock; what the devil, he isnтАЩt a thief!тБатАКтБатАж But if he only knew what was in it. His arm is aching and he is perspiring with the heat. He stops for a moment and puts his burden down on the pavement. Of course he has every intention of returning the wretched thing to its owner; but he would like to question it first. He presses the lock at a venture.тБатАКтБатАж Oh miracle! The two shells open and disclose a pearlтБатАФa pocketbook, which in its turn discloses a bundle of banknotes. Bernard seizes the pearl and shuts up the oyster.

And now that he has the wherewithalтБатАФquick! a hotel. He knows of one close by in the Rue dтАЩAmsterdam. He is dying of hunger. But before sitting down to table, he must put his suitcase in safety. A waiter carries it upstairs before him; three flights; a passage; a door which he locks upon his treasure. He goes down again.

Sitting at table in front of a beefsteak, Bernard did not dare examine the pocketbook. (One never knows who may be watching you.) But his left hand amorously caressed it, lying snug in his inside pocket.

тАЬHow to make Edouard understand that IтАЩm not a thiefтБатАФthatтАЩs the trouble. What kind of fellow is Edouard? Perhaps the suitcase may shed a little light upon that. AttractiveтБатАФso much is certain. But there are heaps of attractive fellows who have no taste for practical joking. If he thinks his suitcase has been stolen, no doubt heтАЩll be glad to see it again. If heтАЩs the least decent heтАЩll be grateful to me for bringing it back to him. I shall easily rouse his interest. LetтАЩs eat the sweet quickly and then go upstairs and examine the situation. Now for the bill and a soul-stirring tip for the waiter.тАЭ

A minute or two later he was back again in his room.

тАЬNow, suitcase, a word with you!тБатАКтБатАж A morning suit, not more than a trifle too big for me, I expect. The material becoming and in good taste. Linen; toilet things. IтАЩm not very sure that I shall give any of all this back. But what proves that IтАЩm not a thief is that these papers interest me a great deal more than anything else. WeтАЩll begin by reading this.тАЭ

This was the notebook into which Edouard had slipped LauraтАЩs melancholy letter. We have already seen the first pages; this is what followed.
